title: Designing RESTful Routes
description: Learn naming conventions and resource nesting.

slides.title: Resource Naming Conventions 📛
slides.content:
# Best Practices for URLs

- Use **nouns**, not verbs (`/users` not `/getUsers`).
- Use plural for collection names (`/users`).
- Use **kebab-case** or **snake_case** consistently (e.g., `/user-profiles`).
- Avoid file extensions (`.json` not needed – use `Accept` header).

Examples:
- `GET /users` – list users
- `GET /users/5` – get user with id 5
- `POST /users` – create new user
- `PUT /users/5` – replace user 5
- `PATCH /users/5` – partially update user 5
- `DELETE /users/5` – delete user 5

slides.title: Nested Resources 🪆
slides.content:
## Representing Relationships

When a resource belongs to another, nest them:

- `GET /users/5/posts` – get all posts by user 5
- `POST /users/5/posts` – create a new post for user 5
- `GET /users/5/posts/3` – get post 3 of user 5

But avoid deep nesting (more than 2 levels) – it can become unwieldy. Consider using query parameters for filtering instead.

slides.title: Query Parameters for Filtering, Sorting, Pagination 🔍
slides.content:
## Using Query Strings

- Filtering: `GET /users?role=admin`
- Sorting: `GET /users?sort=name` or `?sort=-createdAt` (descending)
- Pagination: `GET /users?page=2&limit=20`
- Field selection: `GET /users?fields=id,name,email`

These are not part of the resource path but modify the representation.

quizData.question: Which URL design follows RESTful conventions for getting a specific user's posts?
quizData.explanation: Nesting resources with `/users/5/posts` is a RESTful way to show posts belonging to user 5.
